name: Manny Maceda
draft_email:
Manny, your Davos 2026 insights regarding the shift from AI pilot projects to scaling deployment for bottom-line returns really stood out. It is a transition we are observing closely at Kyber Knight Capital.

While the theoretical potential of AI is clear, the real-world friction lies in integrating these models into legacy commerce stacks. Many firms currently struggle to balance global volatility with the urgent need for productivity gains through actual implementation. We are focusing our pre-seed investments on AI-native tools that bypass these specific labor-intensive bottlenecks.

Given your position at the forefront of this shift, I would love to hear your perspective on the trajectory of labor-adjacent AI deployments. Do you have 30 minutes available next week for a brief conversation? I am happy to work around your schedule or adjust to whatever timing suits you best.

Best,

Alex
Kyber Knight Capital

research_sources.content_summary: Manny Maceda, Chair of Bain & Company, discusses AI deployment at scale and bottom-line impact at WEF 2026.
Confirms Manny Maceda was named Chairman of Bain & Company in February 2025.

claims.claim_text: Manny Maceda emphasized at the 2026 World Economic Forum in Davos that CEOs are shifting their focus from AI pilot projects toward scaling deployment to capture real bottom-line returns.

Bain & Company Bain & Company's headquarters at 350 Boylston St, Boston , Massachusetts Company type Private Industry Management consulting Founded 1973 ; 53 years ago ( 1973 ) [ 1 ] Founder William W. Bain Jr. Patrick F. Graham [ 1 ] [ 2 ] Headquarters Boston , Massachusetts, U.S. Number of locations 64 [ 3 ] Key people Christophe de Vusser (Global Managing Partner) Orit Gadiesh (Chairman) Revenue $7 billion (2025) [ 4 ] Number of employees 22,000 (2025) [ 5 ] Website www .bain .com Bain & Company is an American management consulting company headquartered in Boston , Massachusetts. The firm is one of the largest management consultancy firms in the world and provides advice to public, private and non-profit organizations. [ 6 ] One of the Big Three management consultancies , Bain & Company was founded in 1973 by former Group Vice President of Boston Consulting Group Bill Bain and his colleagues, including Patrick F. Graham. [ 7 ] In the late 1970s and early 1980s, the firm grew rapidly. Bill Bain later spun off the alternative investment business into Bain Capital in 1984 and appointed Mitt Romney as its first chief executive. [ 8 ] Bain experienced several setbacks and financial troubles from 1987 to the early 1990s. Romney and Orit Gadiesh are credited with returning the firm to profitability and growth in their sequential roles as the firm's CEO and chairman respectively. [ 9 ] In the 2000s, Bain & Company continued to expand and create additional practice areas focused on working with non-profits, technology companies, and others. It developed a substantial practice around working with private equity firms. [ 9 ] Corporate history [ edit ] Establishment [ edit ] The idea for Bain & Company was conceived by co-founder William Worthington Bain Jr. during his time at the Boston Consulting Group (BCG). [ 10 ] [ 11 ] In 1970, BCG CEO Bruce Henderson decided to divide his firm into three competing mini-firms: blue, red, and green. Bill Bain and Patrick Graham headed the blue team. [ 12 ] The blue team accounted for over half of BCG's revenue and profits and won the internal competition. After the competition, Bill Bain grew increasingly frustrated by the wait for Henderson's retirement, the firm's project-based approach to consulting, and the refusal of management to help clients execute on the firm's advice. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Around this time, he is quoted to have said to feel like "a consultant on a desert island, writing a report, putting it in a bottle, throwing it in the water, then going on to the next one." [ 12 ] Bain was the expected successor of Henderson within BCG in the early 70s. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] However, in 1973, three years after Henderson's competing team decision, Bill Bain resigned to start his own consulting firm. [ 12 ] [ a ] Most of the senior members of the "blue team" followed him to his newfound company, which was started from his apartment in the Beacon Hill neighborhood of Boston. [ 13 ] A significant part of the firms for which he was responsible at BCG also followed Bain to the new company. Within a few weeks, Bain & Company was working with seven former BCG clients; [ 10 ] this included two of BCG's largest clients, Black & Decker and Texas Instruments . [ 11 ] [ 12 ] [ 14 ] As a result, Henderson accused Bill of stealing BCG's clientele. [ 11 ] It is believed that the competition Henderson put out laid the foundation for Bain & Company. [ 12 ] Bain & Company grew quickly, [ 15 ] primarily through word-of-mouth among CEOs and board members. [ 16 ] The firm established its first formal office in Boston. [ 10 ] This was followed by a European office in London in 1979. [ 10 ] Bain & Company was incorporated in 1985. [ 12 ] The firm grew an average of 50 percent per year, reaching $150 million in revenues by 1986. [ 16 ] The number of staff at the firm tripled from 1980 to 1986, reaching 800 in 1987. [ 16 ] By 1987, Bain & Company was one of the four largest "strategy specialist" consulting firms. [ 15 ] Employee turnover was 8 percent annually compared to an industry average of 20 percent. [ 16 ] Some of the firm's largest clients in this period were National Steel and Chrysler , each of which reduced manufacturing costs with Bain's help. [ 10 ] Turmoil [ edit ] In the late 1980s, Bain & Company experienced a series of setbacks. [ 14 ] A public relations crisis emerged in 1987, due to a controversy involving Bain's work with Guinness . [ 10 ] Tension was growing over the firm's partnership structure, whereby only Bain knew how much the firm was making and decided how much profit-sharing each partner received. [ 16 ] The stock market crashed the same year, and many Bain clients reduced or eliminated their spending with the firm. [ 12 ] There were two rounds of layoffs, eliminating about 30 percent of the workforce. [ 12 ] [ 14 ] [ b ] The Guinness share-trading fraud began with Britain's Department of Trade and Industry investigating whether Bain's client Guinness illegally inflated its stock price. [ 11 ] Bain had helped Guinness trim 150 companies from its portfolio after a period of excessive diversification [ 16 ] and expand into hard liquor with the acquisition of two Scotch whisky companies, [ 10 ] growing profits six-fold. [ 16 ] During this time, Bain made an exception to company policy by allowing a consultant to serve as an interim board member and head of finance for Guinness. [ 11 ] [ 16 ] Bain & Company was not accused of any wrongdoing and no charges were pressed against the firm [ 17 ] for the manipulation of the stock price, but having a Bain consultant work as both vendor and client drew criticisms of its handling of a situation that involved a conflict of interest. [ 11 ] [ 16 ] In 1985 and 1986, Bain & Company took out loans to buy 30 percent of the firm from Bain and other partners for $200 million and used the shares to create an employee stock ownership plan (ESOP). [ 14 ] [ 18 ] These shares of the company were bought at five times Bain & Company's annual revenue, more than double the norm, and cost the firm $25 million in annual interest fees, exacerbating the firm's financial troubles. [ 14 ] Bain hired former U.S. Army general Pete Dawkins as the head of North America in hopes that new leadership could bring about a turnaround, but Dawkins' leadership led to even more turnover. [ 10 ] Bill Bain tried to sell the firm but could not find a buyer. [ 10 ] Rebound [ edit ] Mitt Romney was hired back as interim CEO of Bain & Company in January 1991 [ 18 ] and is credited with saving the company from bankruptcy during his one-year stint in the position. [ 19 ] [ 20 ] Romney had originally left Bain & Company in 1983 after being appointed to lead Bain Capital , [ 12 ] [ c ] an independent private equity firm that would buy companies that Bain & Company partners would improve and re-sell [ 12 ] and whose funds these partners invested in. [ 12 ] Romney allowed managers to know each other's salaries, [ 19 ] re-negotiated the firm's debt, [ 22 ] and restructured the organization so more partners had an ownership stake in the firm. [ 14 ] Romney convinced the founding partners to give up $100 million in equity. [ 18 ] Bain and most of the founding partners left the firm. [ 12 ] Orit Gadiesh Romney left again in December 1992 to pursue a career in politics, [ 10 ] but not before he organized an election the following year that lead to the appointment of Orit Gadiesh as chairman and Thomas J. Tierney as worldwide managing director in July 1993. [ 12 ] [ 23 ] Gadiesh improved morale and loosened the firm's policy against working with companies in the same industry to reduce its reliance on a small number of clients. [ 20 ] London-based Gadiesh has been serving as chairman since. By the end of 1993, Bain & Company was growing once again. [ 13 ] [ 20 ] The firm went from 1,000 employees at its peak, to 550 in 1991, and back up to 800. [ 20 ] The firm opened more offices, including one in New York in 2000. [ 10 ] From 1992 through 1999, the firm grew 25 percent per year and expanded from 12 to 26 offices. [ 24 ] By 1998, the firm had $220 million in annual revenues and 700 staff. [ 11 ] Recent history [ edit ] Bain created two technology consulting practice groups, bainlab and BainNet, in 1999 and 2000 respectively. [ 10 ] bainlab was originally founded as Bain New Venture Group. [ 25 ] It helped startups who otherwise might not afford Bain's fees and accepted partial payment in equity. [ 25 ] In February 2000, Gadiesh was elected for her third consecutive term as the firm's chairman, and Tom Tierney was replaced by John Donahoe as managing director. [ 13 ] Around 2000, the firm became more involved in consulting private equity firms on which companies to invest in and collaborating with technology consulting firms. [ 24 ] By 2005, Bain had the largest share of the market for private equity consulting. [ 26 ] By 2018, Bain's private equity group was over three times as large as that of the next largest consulting firm serving private equity firms and represented 25% of Bain's global business. [ 27 ] Bain & Company does not publish its revenues, but it is estimated to have experienced double-digit annual growth in the 2000s. [ 26 ] Although the market for management consulting was declining, the Big Three management consulting firms , including Bain & Company, continued to grow. [ 28 ] Bain expanded to new offices in other countries, [ 10 ] including India in 2006. [ 29 ] [ 30 ] Like the other big consulting firms, it began working more with governments. [ 28 ] Bain maintained a "generalist" approach to management consulting but created a separate specialist business unit for IT and technology. [ 26 ] In 2012, Robert Bechek was appointed CEO and was later ranked as the most-liked CEO in Glassdoor employee surveys. [ 31 ] On November 20, 2017, Bain announced that Bob Bechek would step down as the worldwide managing director. Manny Maceda , was elected to succeed Bechek as the worldwide managing director effective March 2018. [ 32 ] [ 33 ] In an interview with the Financial Times , Maceda announced a focus on the expansion of Bain's digital practice. "Bain & Company acquired FRWD, a digital marketing agency based in Minneapolis, in 2018. Additionally, it acquired an analytics firm, Pyxis, the following year. Title: Manny Maceda URL Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda Published Time: 2017-11-20T18:05:50Z Markdown Content: From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia | Manny Maceda | | --- | | Born | 1963 (age 62–63) U.S. | | Education | [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology") ([BS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science")) [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") ([MS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master_of_Science "Master of Science")) | | Occupations | [Management Consultant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Management_Consultant "Management Consultant"), [Strategist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strategist "Strategist") | | Employer | [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") | | Title | Chairman | | Spouse | Lyra Rufino | | Children | 4 | **Emmanuel** "**Manny**" **Maceda** (born 1963) is a Filipino-American businessman. As a career [management consultant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Management_consultant "Management consultant"), he was named worldwide managing partner of [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") in November 2017[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-1) and Chairman in February 2025.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) Maceda is [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company")’s first worldwide managing partner of Asian descent.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Bain-3) ## Early life and education [[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Manny_Maceda&action=edit§ion=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")] Manny Maceda was born in the United States and raised in the [Philippines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). He is the eldest son of the late Filipino politician [Ernesto Maceda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernesto_Maceda "Ernesto Maceda"). He earned a bachelor's in chemical engineering from [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"), where he graduated with [magna cum laude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magna_cum_laude "Magna cum laude") honors. He later obtained a masters degree in management from the [MIT Sloan School of Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT_Sloan_School_of_Management "MIT Sloan School of Management").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-named-4) After his studies at the [Illinois Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"), Maceda started his career in the engineering plastics group of [DuPont](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DuPont "DuPont") in 1984. Following this he attended a Master's in Management at [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT "MIT").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) During his master's at [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT "MIT") he was still "unaware of the existence of the management-consulting industry." While at Sloan, he persuaded one of his role models, [Jack Welch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jack_Welch "Jack Welch"), the chairman and chief executive of General Electric Co., to speak at the school. During the speech, a man wearing a gas mask threw a grenade-like object at Welch's feet. He stopped speaking and Maceda, correctly assessing that the object wasn't a real grenade, picked it up and walked it out of the building.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-WSJ-6) Sometime later, Welch invited Maceda to lunch and introduced him to two top GE executives, the head of corporate strategy and the head of plastics marketing, [Jeffrey Immelt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeffrey_Immelt "Jeffrey Immelt"), who would later become GE's chairman and CEO. Both offered Maceda jobs, but he decided to join Bain instead.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-WSJ-6) In 1988, Maceda joined Bain as a consultant. He specializes in "full potential" transformations involving strategy, performance improvement, customer loyalty and organizational effectiveness that result in meaningful long-term increase in enterprise value. He has worked with clients across a range of industries, including financial services, industrial products, consumer products, retail, telecommunications, energy and technology.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-GNW-7) Before being appointed as the new worldwide director, Maceda led Bain's global Full Potential Transformation practice and was a member of the firm's Management Committee, Global Operating Committee and Nominating Committee. Previously he served as chairman of Bain's Asia-Pacific region. Before this he was a global leader of the Performance Improvement and Reengineering practices and leader of the global recruiting team.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-named-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) Meanwhile, he also published articles, such as in the MIT World Series, and spoke at the [World Economic Forum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum").[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-8) In November 2017, Maceda was named the successor of [Bob Bechek](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bob_Bechek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bob Bechek (page does not exist)") as the worldwide managing director of [Bain & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") when Bechek stepped down in March 2018.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-FT-5) Maceda stated that he wanted to focus on the development of Bain's digital practice. Maceda stepped down from the CEO role in summer 2024.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) In February 2025, Maceda was named as the successor of [Orit Gadiesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orit_Gadiesh "Orit Gadiesh") as the Chairman of Bain & Company.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manny_Maceda#cite_note-Fortune-2) He is a member of the MIT Sloan North American Advisory Board. Maceda is married to Lyra of the [Rufino family](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rufino_family "Rufino family"). Business News WEF 2026 | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda on why the world is long on India WEF 2026 | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda on why the world is long on India Manny Maceda, Chair of Bain & Company, says AI is beginning to show bottom-line impact in specific functions like software engineering and customer care, with wider gains from productivity and revenue growth expected to materialise over the next one to three years as adoption scales. By Shereen Bhan January 22, 2026, 5:55:20 PM IST (Published) 9 Min Read Speaking to CNBC-TV18 on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum 2026 in Davos, Manny Maceda , Chair of Bain & Company , strikes an optimistic yet pragmatic note on India’s place in a rapidly fragmenting global order. Rejecting the idea of a rigid US-China binary, Maceda positions India as a major power in its own right—one that is benefiting from its ability to balance multiple bilateral relationships while strengthening its domestic market, infrastructure, and capabilities in areas such as AI and energy. He underscores that India has become one of the highest-priority markets for multinational corporations and global investors, even overtaking China in foreign direct investment. For Bain, this translates into a sustained commitment to India—not just as a fast-growing client market, but also as a global talent and capability hub. At the same time, Maceda flags the disruptive potential of AI for global capability centres, calling for foresight and resilience as companies navigate technological and geopolitical uncertainty. Read More | Bain & Company's Manny Maceda says CEOs must rethink playbooks for a fragmented world order Below are the edited excerpts of the interview. Q: Talking about India in the context of Carney's medium power thesis, do you believe the medium powers or countries who not firmly placed between this US China battle will, in fact, benefit from what is going on today? A: I would probably place India squarely. I would not consider you a small power, the world's largest population in the world, a very attractive market. So just the recognition of that, and I don't know that the world is as simple as it's two poles, and where are you? I guess that's part of his thesis. You have to play multiple sides. You have to understand and so the fact that India is building, let us call it multiple bilateral relationships with different countries, it's arguably, it's a good thing and it's the right thing, and you have to, you have to play that and balance it. By the way those are country strategies, companies are doing versions of that strategy too, because it goes to, well, where do I do business, which, which suppliers do I go to, where do I hire and move my talent? And for all that's happening, it's you and I have talked the last years India is a high priority market for the multinational corporates and private investors, relative to the other markets in the world. It is a great domestic market. It's upgrading its capabilities and infrastructure, in AI, in energy. China used to be higher foreign direct investment than India. Well, India passed China. And most people I know are still long on India, us included. Q: I know Bain is long on India, what does that mean as far as Bain’s plans for India? A: I think continuing to have it be a priority market to keep building and so if you think of any market that we are in as a professional global services firm number one is the domestic market. Indian companies are growing large, and they are operating not just in India, but they are going around the world. Number two, multinationals, they prioritise India. Number three, our corporate, our financial investor base, private equity firms, most of them will say, this is one of our top two or three markets in the world. And so if you say all of that, are places that we can help support and enable for India to grow, we will be there by showing up with more people, more markets. Also like many companies, we have a large capability centre here and that's why we have the highest headcount in India, after the United States. That's going to continue to be a good place, although that's also an interesting place. Going back to your first question, the impact of AI and tasks performed by Indian capability centres is probably something you want to think about and anticipate some disruption as well. But as far as we are concerned, we'll continue to invest in India, and I will keep coming to visit and we can keep having these chats. Q: Do you walk away from Davos as a concerned optimist or a cautious optimist? A: I am an optimist still. I legitimately do have concerns. This is a complicated time in the world, and when you combine the implications of global politics, the potential of AI, and even some of the things we haven't talked about, the intersection of those two, not just on things like combat, defence, weaponry capabilities, drones, security. hacking, etc. there's a lot you could be concerned about that we have also lived through and so building our resilient muscles. If we can scenario plan, multiple worst-case scenarios, and have an agenda that we do a few things to so that we are ready when they do happen, I think we will be better off. Q: You have been engaging with CEOs both in the lead-up to Davos and during the meetings here. What is the prevailing sentiment among them? Are business leaders saying they cannot afford to wait for global uncertainty—whether linked to President Trump or other factors—to ease, and must continue executing their plans? A: I would say that the thematic issues that CEOs have wrestled with actions haven't changed that much the last three or four years. AI is number one, the post global order and then we are in the middle of that shift is number two. And then on top of that, the more ongoing challenge of energy transition during scarcer higher cost of capital times. But the first and the second are the most intertwined. AI is now at the point where it's real are we deploying it at scale and the post global order, whatever that is or that means, intersects in ways that influences all of our not just our strategic choices, but our strategic scenario and contingency planning. Q: AI continues to be top of mind here and as you said, it's real. It is being deployed. We are moving now from pilot to deployment at scale. But the question still being asked is, what is it doing to the bottomline? It's not showing up on the bottom line yet. When will it start to show up in the bottom line? A: If you look at the valuation of companies, obviously the expectation is that all of these values will show up. I would say that probably 20% are already getting it into the bottomline. And it started with where does the bottomline show up? The most obvious places is it is not across the board, it's in very specific functions, tasks that and so when you have a lot of tasks that could be AI enabled in a way that the productivity is increased, that shows up some basic software engineering, customer care. In many cases, it hasn't scaled to more complicated tasks yet, because it's an organisational change, people have to adopt it and use it to capture it. On the flip side, whether it will show up, being underwritten with revenue growth and new ideas and new opportunities., both of these haven't happened yet at scale, but I would say the expectation, the optimism, the belief that all of these investments we are making will show up is very high and that it will be in the next one to three years. Q: Are CEOs still focused on AI from an optimisation point of view, from an efficiency point of view, or are we closer to using AI from really transformation purposes? A: I would say it's probably 50-50, and so if you look, well, what does transformation mean anyway for a company. I would say it is half and half that the easiest way to underwrite your use case. You are making a meaningful investment in additional technology spend in AI well, where will that come from? The most obvious case is, can I redeploy my past technology spend to it? The second is, can I get productivity out of it, back to these specific and does productivity mean labour efficiency that I can capture? That's a little bit where most of them started. The term transformation is hard, because native to AI companies that will start just like in past technologies might get there faster, quicker than an existing company that has to transform. But that is that probably the number one topic that's still discussed here. Now there is an overlap between AI and all the global issues. It shows up in the new technology stack, that is the AI stack of today because we know what the old stack was, what was the semiconductor? What was the PC, what was the software, what was the applications layer? The new stack starts with things that you didn't think of as part of the stack before, predictable, reliable energy, a lot of it. For data centres, big cloud service providers that had been mostly American companies a new layer called the large language model that's playing out. So, I do think the companies that are now deciding they will continue to invest in AI with the belief and expectation that we will capture the returns to that investment, either in cost efficiency or new markets. But it has to overlap with your geographic choices. Bain and Company, which Manny led as Worldwide Managing Partner up until recently, when he transitioned to Chairman, was also ranked #1 in Glassdoor’s 2024 Best Paces to Work list, and has been the top-ranked consulting firm on Glassdoor for ten years in a row. Under Manny’s leadership, Bain has also excelled financially. Between 2018 and 2023, revenue increased by more than 75%, outpacing McKinsey. A global firm operating in 22 countries, Bain’s culture is remarkably consistent. Its culture ranked #1 across consulting firms in 18 of those countries. “A Bainie never lets another Bainie fail” is core to the firm’s values. Sure enough, on Glassdoor Bain employees speak considerably more frequently and positively about organizational support than any other consulting firm, one of a wide variety of cultural dimensions they significantly outperform peers on. An MIT alumnus, Manny joined Bain in 1989 and in his 35 year career at Bain advised CEOs at some of the world’s most successful companies. In this episode, Manny reflects on the role of culture as the operating system coordinating Bain’s success, how Bain successfully lives up to its core values over a long time horizon, and how the company maintains its culture so consistently across a global organization. Elite Career Platform: Bain Careers offer strong mentorship, rapid responsibility, and highly competitive compensation comparable to other MBB firms. Rigorous Case Interviews: The Bain interview process emphasizes structured thinking, clear communication, and strong business judgment. Bain & Company is one of the world’s leading management consulting firms and the youngest of the MBB firms (McKinsey, BCG, Bain). Founded in 1973, Bain is known for its focus on measurable client results, strong private equity practice, and distinctive team-oriented culture. The firm advises corporations, private equity funds, and public sector organizations on strategy, transformation, performance improvement, and growth initiatives. Bain & Company was named a 2026 Top Ranked Consulting Firm by Management Consulted . With 67 offices worldwide and more than 19,000 employees, Bain operates globally while maintaining a locally anchored staffing model and strong office identity. Key Stats Website www.bain.com Headquarters Boston, MA Employees 19,000+ professionals Offices 67 offices across 40+ countries Worldwide Managing Partner Christophe De Vusser Annual Revenue ~$7B (estimated) Bain and Company Careers Bain & Company operates as a global partnership serving corporate, private equity, and public sector clients across industries and functional domains. Consultants are aligned with a home office and typically staffed on projects within that region, though cross-office collaboration occurs for specialized expertise and global engagements. The firm is known for its strong private equity advisory platform and results-oriented approach to client work. Bain emphasizes measurable impact, often working closely with senior leadership teams on growth strategy, transformation, and performance improvement initiatives. Project teams are typically lean, providing junior consultants with early responsibility and direct client exposure. Check out their career's page for more information. Bain Jobs Bain Jobs include roles such as Associate Consultant, Consultant, Manager, Principal, and Partner. The firm also hires Advanced Degree Candidates, experienced industry hires, and functional experts in digital, analytics, and implementation. For current openings, candidates can visit Bain’s official careers page or explore opportunities on the Management Consulted Job Board . Practice Areas Bain advises clients across a broad range of functional capabilities. Core practice areas include: Strategy – Corporate strategy, growth strategy, portfolio strategy, and competitive positioning Private Equity & M&A – Commercial due diligence, value creation, portfolio strategy, and post-acquisition transformation Performance Improvement – Cost transformation, operational excellence, procurement, and margin expansion Customer & Commercial Excellence – Pricing, customer experience, brand strategy, and sales effectiveness Organization & Leadership – Organizational design, talent strategy, and operating model transformation Digital & Technology – Digital strategy, advanced analytics, AI, and technology-enabled transformation Sustainability & ESG – Climate strategy, decarbonization, and sustainable growth initiatives Transformation – End-to-end enterprise transformations and large-scale change programs Bain is particularly recognized for the scale of its private equity advisory practice and its work in performance improvement and enterprise transformation. Industries Bain serves clients across most major global industries, including: Aerospace & Defense Automotive & Mobility Consumer Products Energy & Natural Resources Financial Services Healthcare Industrial Goods & Services Media & Entertainment Private Equity Retail Technology Telecommunications Transportation & Logistics Utilities & Renewables Public & Social Sector Industry focus can vary by office, as Bain’s staffing model is often locally anchored. Offices typically develop strong expertise aligned with regional client demand. Bain Office Locations Bain operates 67 offices across 40+ countries in North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Latin America. The firm maintains a locally anchored staffing model. Consultants are aligned with a home office and are most frequently staffed on projects within that region. While cross-office collaboration occurs – particularly for global clients or specialized expertise – Bain places strong emphasis on office identity and local team cohesion. Several offices, including Boston, San Francisco, London, and New York, are among the firm’s largest. In recent decades, Bain has expanded significantly across Europe and Asia-Pacific, with more than half of firm revenue generated outside North America. Recruiting standards are consistent globally, though industry exposure may vary by office depending on regional client demand. Bain Career Path Bain follows a structured, performance-based promotion model. Advancement is based on demonstrated impact, leadership, problem-solving ability, and contribution to clients and the firm. While timelines vary, progression typically follows this path: Associate Consultant (Undergraduate entry) Senior Associate Consultant Consultant (MBA or advanced degree entry) Case Team Leader Principal Partner Bain operates under an “up or out” system, though the firm emphasizes mentorship and development to support progression. Consultants are typically staffed on one project at a time, allowing for deep engagement and clear accountability. Team sizes are often lean, which can lead to early responsibility and client exposure. Externship & Rotation Opportunities Bain offers a structured externship program for Associate Consultants and Consultants after approximately two years at the firm. Options may include: Temporary placement at another Bain office Private equity rotations within Bain’s Private Equity Group Externships at startups or corporate clients Opportunities with The Bridgespan Group (Bain’s nonprofit affiliate) Participants return to Bain after the externship period. This program is designed to broaden experience while retaining high-performing consultants within the firm. Exit Opportunities Bain alumni pursue leadership roles across private equity, corporate strategy, operating roles, startups, and public sector organizations. Given Bain’s strength in private equity advisory and performance improvement, many alumni transition into: Private equity portfolio operations roles Private equity investing roles Corporate strategy and business unit leadership Operating partner positions Venture-backed startups and entrepreneurship MBA or advanced degree programs Bain maintains a strong alumni network, particularly within private equity and growth-focused organizations. Former consultants often move into roles requiring strategic clarity, operational transformation experience, and hands-on value creation. Some alumni choose to remain within the broader Bain ecosystem, including opportunities with The Bridgespan Group or other affiliated initiatives. Notable Alumni Here's a list of notable Bain alum: Kenneth Chenault, CEO of American Express Gary Crittenden, CFO of Citigroup, Monsanto and Sears John Donahoe, CEO of eBay Meg Whitman, CEO of Hewlett Packard Mark Pincus (Bain Intern), Founder and CEO of Zynga Jonathan Kraft, President of the New England Patriots Mitt Romney, Former Governor of Massachusetts, Co-Founder of Bain Capital, 2012 Republican nominee for President of the United States Vivek Paul, Partner at Texas Pacific Group, former CEO of Wipro Javed Ahmed, Chief Executive of Tate & Lyle Jeffrey Zients, Chief Performance Office, Executive Office of the President of the U.S. Bain and Company History Bain & Company was founded in 1973 by Bill Bain and six former colleagues from Boston Consulting Group. From its inception, the firm positioned itself differently by emphasizing measurable client results rather than delivering reports alone. Bain’s early focus on long-term client relationships and outcome-driven advisory work helped it grow rapidly through the late 1970s and early 1980s. During the 1980s, the firm expanded significantly but later encountered financial strain tied to its internal ownership and capital structure. In the early 1990s, Mitt Romney – who had joined Bain in the late 1970s and later co-founded Bain Capital in 1984 – returned to help stabilize Bain & Company’s operations as interim CEO. The restructuring strengthened the firm’s financial footing and clarified its governance model. It is important to distinguish between Bain & Company, the management consulting firm, and Bain Capital, the private equity firm. While both were founded by Bill Bain and share historical ties, they operate as separate legal entities with independent ownership and governance. Bain & Company does perform advisory work for private equity firms – including Bain Capital – but the organizations are distinct. In 1993, Bain formalized its leadership structure by separating the Chairman and Worldwide Managing Director roles. Orit Gadiesh became Chairman in 1993 and has remained in that role for decades, providing continuity during the firm’s global expansion. Through the 2000s and 2010s, Bain broadened its capabilities beyond traditional strategy into transformation, private equity advisory, performance improvement, and sustainability. The firm expanded its geographic footprint significantly, growing to 67 offices worldwide. Today, Bain & Company advises clients globally across strategy, private equity, transformation, and performance improvement initiatives. Bain and Company Culture Bain is widely recognized for its team-oriented and relationship-driven culture. While expectations are high and performance standards are rigorous, the firm places strong emphasis on collaboration, mentorship, and local office cohesion. Team Environment Bain’s staffing model reinforces its office-centered culture. Manny Maceda's Journey of Courage | Aconic Podcast Home Episodes News About Contact Home Episodes News About Contact From Grenades to Gratitude: Manny Maceda’s Journey of Courage, Purpose, and Leadership Ahmed January 7, 2025 Leaping towards a “grenade” for Jack Welch is not a standard business school experience, but Manny Maceda is not a standard person. In a discussion with Aconic, Manny shares his experience coming to the US after his family faced extreme economic and political duress in the Philippines, finding his early bearings at Dupont and business school, and ultimately rising to lead iconic consulting firm Bain and Company. While I never had the opportunity to work under Manny’s leadership (having started my career at another consulting firm), just hearing his journey was galvanizing. Three themes of the discussion especially stood out: courage, gratitude and purpose. From the Jack Welch “grenade” incident (this story alone is worth the listen), to agreeing to stay in America with his father, persistently pursuing the Bain top role after initial disappointment, evolving the company’s mission statement: Manny has demonstrated no shortage of courage in his personal life and career. “We all will get disappointments, it’s a matter of how you shake yourself off and be grateful for the opportunity” Manny reflects. In all facets of life, Manny exemplifies deep gratitude for the gifts he has been given and the people around him. In turn, this gratitude underpins an astonishing degree of positivity. Not everyone is so appreciative of their first employer, but Manny’s gratitude for Dupont is clear. His intentional choice to ‘not dwell too much on the downsides’ is infectious. “We’re all dealt a set of cards. Let’s be grateful for what we have. Let’s do what we can with it.” Purpose was another prominent theme throughout the discussion. Manny calls-out the value of taking time to write a personal mission statement. Recalling the statement he crafted when elected as a Bain partner, he emphasizes the personal importance of building a great family and firm for the world. To that end, Manny shared a few reflections on a path forward for the Asian American and Pacific Islander communities… started with celebrating and being grateful for the heritage. “Part of the positivity and goodness is to define being Asian American and Pacific Islander as to be proud of, as something that’s a position of strength.” Through his example of courage, gratitude, purpose and more, Manny provides a burst of inspiration. You can hear the full, extraordinary discussion here . FBV, as a part of Bain & Company, has access to a range of resources and expertise that are different from and complementary to the platform of a traditional venture fund. Our portfolio companies gain access to the powerful Bain platform in a way that is tailored to their stage and needs. For example, we bring Bain’s industry insights, advisory support, global network, audience, and access to talent. This unique combination gives our portfolio companies a competitive edge in navigating challenges and achieving accelerated success. Our team structure, operations, diligence, and evaluation process are similar to what you’d find at a traditional venture fund. Is it too early to reach out to FBV? Absolutely not! We are always eager to engage with innovative founders at any stage of their journey and provide support along the way. Whether you’re still in the ideation phase, developing your product, or not yet fundraising, we welcome the opportunity to have a conversation with you. Feel free to set up time with someone on our team here . Let's start the dialogue early and build a relationship that can grow as your start-up evolves. Do you invest in start-ups that are not led by Bain alumni? Yes, we do. While our primary focus is on supporting Bain alumni, we also recognize the value and potential in start-ups led by non-Bain alumni. In select cases where we see that our involvement, along with the resources of Bain & Company, can be instrumental in accelerating the start-up’s success, we are open to considering an investment. We believe in fostering innovation and are always on the lookout for exceptional teams and groundbreaking ideas, regardless of their alumni status. What stages do you invest in? Our first checks are typically in start-ups from the pre-seed stage up to Series B, though we aim to follow-on in later rounds. Our goal is to support early-stage companies through their initial growth phases. What industries do you invest in? FBV is a generalist fund, meaning we invest across a diverse range of industries. Our broad expertise, combined with Bain & Company's comprehensive industry knowledge, allows us to identify and support high-potential start-ups across various sectors, including technology, financial services, consumer goods, industrials, and beyond. Do you lead rounds? Typically, no. We believe in a partnership model wherein another venture fund leads a round and we participate as a co-investor. This allows us to complement the lead investors by bringing our unique set of resources and expertise to the table. What kind of support can portfolio companies expect from FBV? Beyond financial investment, portfolio companies can expect strategic and operational support from FBV and Bain & Company. We provide advisory support via Bain partners, access to Bain IP, introductions to relevant contacts, promotion through FBV and Bain channels, and access to Bain. Our goal is to act as a strategic partner, helping founders navigate challenges and capitalize on opportunities by bringing the best of Bain. Subscribe for updates Want to stay in touch?
